problem_summary: 2014 MCM Problem B (discrete): Sports Illustrated wants the best all-time college coach(es) — male or female, past or present — across any sport. The team had to build a metric to rank coaches, handle the effect of time-line horizon (eras across ~70 years of NCAA history) and gender, and demonstrate the model on at least 3 sports plus write a non-technical Sports Illustrated article. This paper (Team 26636) frames it as a multi-attribute ranking problem solved with a weight-determination stage plus a grey relational ranking stage, then adds an era-correction factor for winning percentage.
models_used: Grey Relational Analysis (GRA / grey correlation analysis) as the core ranking engine: each coach's 4-factor vector is compared against an 'ideal coach' reference vector (component-wise best values); grey relational coefficient with distinguishing coefficient rho=0.5, then weighted sum into a grey relational degree r_i used to rank

Fuzzy Consistent Matrix method for subjective weight determination: build a fuzzy preferential relation matrix A from pairwise 0/0.5/1 importance judgments, convert to a fuzzy consistent matrix F via f_ij=(f_i - f_j)/(2m)+0.5, then extract weights with the root (geometric-mean) method — yielding w=(NCAA champions .3527 wait-by-factor, Pct .2854, Wins .2169-style, Coach Awards .1450)

Shannon's Entropy method (objective MADM weighting) used as a cross-check/robustness alternative to the fuzzy matrix weights; produced near-uniform weights ~0.25 each and an almost-identical top-10 list

One-way ANOVA (single-factor analysis of variance) to test whether mean winning percentage differs significantly across five era groups, and to confirm Wooden/Rupp as statistical outliers (P jumps from 0.05-significant to 0.08 after removing them)

Weighted Moving Average method to build the era 'correction factor' alpha_i = 1/S_i (S_i = sample-size-weighted mean winning percentage of a group) that deflates old coaches' Pct and inflates modern coaches' Pct before re-ranking
key_techniques: Two-stage decomposition: separate the weighting problem (fuzzy consistent matrix) from the ranking problem (GRA), so each stage can be validated independently

Universal 4-factor scheme deliberately chosen to transfer across sports and genders: Wins, Winning Percentage (Pct), NCAA Championships, Coach-of-the-Year Awards — making the same model reusable for basketball, football, ice hockey and women's basketball with only the award/championship definitions swapped
'Ideal coach' reference series: GRA needs a reference; they synthesize a fictitious best-in-class coach (component-wise maxima) and measure each real coach's grey closeness to it
0-1 min-max normalization of all factors before GRA to remove unit/scale mismatch

Era-stratification: bucket coaches into 5 time groups (pre-1930s, 30s-40s, 50s-60s, 70s-80s, 90s-2000s) and quantify the downward Pct trend, then justify a correction factor only where ANOVA supports it (groups 3-5)

Outlier handling driven by statistics, not intuition: ANOVA identifies Wooden and Rupp as extreme values inflating early-era variance; they are treated separately (correction applied only post-1950, qualitative deflation pre-1950)

Layered validation: external validity (compare top-5 vs ESPN / Bleacher Report / Yahoo / Inside College Hockey), stability test (swap weighting method to entropy; swap the importance ordering of factors), and sensitivity test (vary rho = 0.4 / 0.5 / 0.6) — top-10 essentially unchanged each time
Full reproducibility: MATLAB code for all three pipelines (fuzzy weights, entropy weights, full GRA) included in the appendix with the raw data tables
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins on three axes that O-papers are scored on. (1) Direct, complete responsiveness: it nails every clause of the prompt — a rankable metric, explicit time-line-horizon analysis with a quantitative fix, an explicit gender analysis, demonstration on 3 sports, and the required Sports Illustrated article. Nothing is hand-waved. (2) Methodological honesty and self-skepticism: rather than asserting its answer, it stress-tests it three ways (entropy re-weighting, importance re-ordering, rho sensitivity) and benchmarks the output against four independent professional rankings, showing the top names (Wooden, Rupp, Krzyzewski, Smith) reproduce. Judges reward a model that survives the team's own attack. (3) An original, well-motivated 'shining point': the era-correction factor. They noticed winning percentage is era-biased (old coaches faced weaker fields), proved the trend with ANOVA, isolated outliers statistically, and corrected only where the data justified it via a weighted-moving-average factor. That is exactly the kind of insight-plus-rigor the prompt's time-line clause was fishing for. The fit between small, noisy, heterogeneous data and the deliberate choice of grey relational analysis (designed for small-sample, poor-information systems) also reads as a mature, defensible modeling decision rather than a default."
section_highlights: "Strong structural choices a future team can copy: (1) A summary page that states the whole pipeline in order (fuzzy matrix -> weights -> GRA -> rank -> validate) and names the 'shining point' (era correction) up front, so a triage judge sees the contribution in 30 seconds. (2) A clean Model section that fully separates notation/definitions from 'Model Designing,' written as numbered Process I (weights) and Process II (ranking) — algorithmic and easy to audit. (3) Application section walks one sport (men's basketball) end-to-end with every intermediate table shown (raw data -> normalized arrays -> grey coefficients -> grey degree -> final rank), then generalizes to football/hockey by only changing the factor definitions — demonstrating reusability concretely. (4) Dedicated, clearly-headed sections for the two prompt sub-questions (Section 5 time-line horizon, Section 6 gender) so the judge can check the rubric directly. (5) Validation is its own visible block: Section 4 external comparison, Section 7 stability (two sub-tests), Section 8 sensitivity. (6) Honest Strengths/Weaknesses section that names real limitations (subjective award counts, the >200-wins cutoff excluding some greats, no clean pre-1950 correction). (7) Appendix with full raw data and runnable MATLAB makes it reproducible. The English is imperfect but the logical scaffolding is excellent."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Subjectivity smuggled into a 'quantitative' model: the fuzzy preferential matrix encodes the team's own ordering (NCAA champions > Pct > Wins > Coach Awards) via 0/0.5/1 judgments — the entropy cross-check actually produced near-equal ~0.25 weights, showing the headline weights are largely a human prior, not data-driven

Inconsistent reported results: the men's basketball #1 flips between John Wooden (Tables 5, 13, the SI article) and Mike Krzyzewski (Tables 4 and the rho sensitivity Tables 21-23), and the football top-5 ordering in the SI article (Bowden #4) contradicts the model table (Bear Bryant #1, Bowden #4) vs Section 4 (Bowden #1) — a real internal-consistency defect a sharp judge would catch

Coach Awards factor is admittedly arbitrary: which awards are counted (and that older coaches simply could not win awards that did not exist yet, e.g. trophies starting in 1966) biases the metric, an era-bias they correct for Pct but not for awards or championships
Eligibility cutoffs (>600 wins for basketball, >200 for football) mechanically exclude legendary short-tenure or older coaches (e.g. Knute Rockne), which they concede weakens 'all-time' claims

The pre-1950 era correction is hand-waved ('because of the scarcity of data, we cannot use a precise formulation') — they apply a rigorous weighted-moving-average factor only post-1950 and a vague qualitative deflation before that

GRA's 'ideal coach' = component-wise maxima can be dominated by a single extreme coach (Wooden's 10 championships sets the champions scale), compressing everyone else and making the ranking sensitive to one outlier
After applying the correction factor, grey relational degrees exceed 1 and Pct values exceed 1 (Table 14), i.e. the normalization no longer holds post-correction — mathematically untidy
The appendix MATLAB has transcription errors (e.g. powerprod, a dangling '+' line), so the 'reproducible' code would not run as printed
reusable_patterns: Weights-then-rank two-stage MADM template: use a weighting method (fuzzy consistent matrix OR entropy) to set factor weights, then a distance/closeness method (GRA, or TOPSIS) against an ideal reference to produce the ranking — clean separation that generalizes to any 'evaluate/rank N entities on K criteria' MCM problem

Subjective-vs-objective weighting cross-check as a built-in robustness argument: compute weights both ways (fuzzy/AHP-style AND Shannon entropy) and show the ranking is invariant — a cheap, persuasive stability test judges love
Sensitivity sweep on the one tunable hyperparameter (here rho in {0.4,0.5,0.6}) and report that the ranking is unchanged — a reusable template for defending any model with a 'magic constant'

External-benchmark validation: when ground truth is unavailable, compare your ranking against several independent authoritative lists (ESPN/Yahoo/etc.) and show agreement on the top tier — applicable to any subjective ranking task

Confounder-correction pattern: detect a bias variable (era), prove it with ANOVA across stratified groups, then build a data-driven correction factor (weighted moving average) and re-rank — a transferable recipe for any 'past vs present is unfair' adjustment

Demonstrate transferability by design: pick criteria abstract enough that switching domains only requires redefining the inputs (swap which awards/championships count), then actually run the model on 2-3 domains to prove it
Statistical outlier isolation: when a few entities distort variance, remove them, re-run ANOVA, and let the P-value confirm they are outliers before treating them specially

Deliverable discipline: include a runnable code appendix + full raw-data tables for reproducibility, and write the required popular-audience piece (the SI article) in plain, enthusiastic prose with the headline rankings — judges check that the communication deliverable is actually present and readable
